text: The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum and identifies the ureter. Gentle dissection reveals the anterior division of the internal iliac artery or the origin of the uterine artery.
The assistant retracts the uterus, allowing the surgeon to apply uterine clips on the anterior division of the internal iliac artery, with the ureter visibly positioned far from the surgical field.

The surgeon performs dissection on the opposite side with the assistant retracting the uterus. The uterine artery is isolated and dissected, with the ureter visibly far from the uterine artery. Bilateral uterine artery clipping is performed under direct vision.
Following bilateral uterine artery clipping, the vascularity of the uterus visibly decreases, resulting in minimal blood loss during the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
The uterus appears white due to reduced vascularity. The surgeon begins the procedure by making incisions on the most prominent aspects of the fibroids using a laparoscopic shearer.

The surgeon removes the first fibroid, followed by the extraction of a large posterior fibroid. Despite the fibroid's size, minimal bleeding is observed at the base, highlighting the effectiveness of prior uterine artery clipping.

The surgeon observes the large fibroid base with minimal bleeding due to bilateral uterine artery ligation. The next fibroid is removed, and after all fibroids are enucleated, uterine reconstruction is performed using quill sutures.
The surgeon completes the myomectomy by removing the fibroids through morcellation, demonstrating the effectiveness of the technique in achieving the procedure with minimal blood loss.
